coredata.dc:title: Sustainable gambogic acid release via pH/Redox Dual-Responsive C60-Modified magnetic mesoporous nanospheres for antitumor therapy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

coredata.dc:creator: Huan, Wei-wei
Dong, Mengyang
Chang, Ziling
Li, Huafei
Liu, Wei
Yang, Yuxiang
Yuan, Hongmin
Huang, Yan
Liu, Wenwen
Carlini, Riccardo
Chen, Mo
coredata.dc:description: Traditional cancer treatment modalities such as surgery, chemotherapy, and radiation therapy have seen significant advancements in the past. However, inherent drawbacks, including systemic toxicity and high recurrence rates. Consequently, The nanoparticles, with their targeting and multifunctionality, have captured significant attention. This work synthesizes HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI (HMNPs:Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-C18) nanoparticles, integrating diverse functionalities for enhanced cancer therapy. The inclusion of SiO2 and C60 significantly improves the loading efficiency of gallic acid (GA). The magnetic properties of HMNPs enable efficient targeting to cancer sites under external magnetic fields. The -SS- linkage ensures drug release exclusively in tumor cells over-expressing glutathione (GSH), minimizing adverse effects on normal tissues. Introduction of Polyethyleneimine (PEI) imparts positive charge to facilitate accumulation on negatively charged tumor surfaces and enhances biocompatibility. Additionally, the fluorescence properties of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) facilitate real-time monitoring of material distribution in vivo during mouse and clinical therapy. Furthermore, the nano-magnetic targeting material demonstrates efficient drug loading and precise control over drug release, addressing issues such as the poor biocompatibility and high biotoxicity associated with traditional materials. This study not only achieves effective tumor treatment but also offers a promising direction for developing efficient multifunctional nano-targeting materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spherical material
pH response
Redox response
Magnetic targeted therapy
Gambogic acid

Zeta potential analysis of composite mesoporous particles
Structural analysis of composite mesoporous particles
Drug loading and sustained release properties of composite mesoporous microparticles

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 20577010 , 20971043 ), the Open Project Program of the State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, Jilin University and the Three agricultures and nine agricultural related institutions\u201D Agricultural Science and Technology Cooperation Plan Project of Zhejiang Province ( 2022SNJF059 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (Scheme. 1) Schematic illustration of the synthesis and induced pyroptosis process of HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI nanoparticles for cancer targeted therapy. Spherical TEM of (a) α-Fe2O3, insets are α-Fe2O3@SiO2 TEM images. (b) HMNPs; (c)SEM of HMNPs, (d) HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs, (e) HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI and EDS Mapping analysis of each element.

(a) N2 adsorption and desorption isotherm, (b) pore size distribution of spherical HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI, (c) Hysteresis loops (e)Fluorescence spectra (f) Zeta-potential of spherical carrier materials at different stages, (d) UV absorption and fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs.
(a-e) XPS survey spectrum (f) XRD of spherical carrier materials at different stages.

Cumulative sustained release rate of (a) spherical HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI; MTT Cytotoxicity analysis was performed on SMMC-7721 cells treated with various concentrations of (b) spherical HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs (GA) and (c) HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI (GA) for 12 h, 48 h, and 72 h. (d) Encapsulation rate of GA (e) the release rate at pH = 5.7 + GSH by carrier materials at different stages.

(a) HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI treated SMMC-7721cells laser confocal image, (b) tumor pictures in the magnetic targeting drug administration group, (c) Tissue sections of the heart, spleen and lung of the spherical carrier of the control group-a and the magnetically targeted drug delivery group-b, (d) Tumor growth inhibition under diverse treatment conditions employing spherical carriers.
SPECT and MIP image of SMMC-7721 Tumor-Bearing Mouse.
Cumulative sustained release rate of spherical HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI(GA) under different conditions.

head.ce:title: Sustainable gambogic acid release via pH/Redox Dual-Responsive C60-Modified magnetic mesoporous nanospheres for antitumor therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Zhejiang A&F University, Hangzhou, Zhejiang 311300, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Zhejiang A&F University, Hangzhou, Zhejiang 311300, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Lifesciences, Shanghai University, 333 Nanchen Road, Shanghai, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Lifesciences, Shanghai University, 333 Nanchen Road, Shanghai, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry and Material Chemistry Department, LAS Klee-Barabino, 16146 Genova, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry and Material Chemistry Department, LAS Klee-Barabino, 16146 Genova, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In our work, we first synthesized a magnetic mesoporous silica nanoparticle as a precursor to drug carrier. To enhance the carrier's drug loading capacity and impart multifunctionality, sequential incorporation of C60, N-GQDs, -SS-, and PEI was undertaken. This led to the synthesis of spherical drug carrier nanoparticles denoted as HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI. The grafting of various functional groups has increased the drug loading capacity of the material. Introduction of -SS- enables it to possess redox-responsive controlled release capability, while N-GQDs impart excellent fluorescence properties to the material. Finally, we injected this material into the tails of mice and utilized external magnetic targeting to transport it to tumor sites. Upon interaction with the overexpressed acidity and GSH in tumor cells, the material's structure ruptures, enabling precise drug release and reducing adverse effects on the organism.

Traditional cancer treatment modalities such as surgery, chemotherapy, and radiation therapy have seen significant advancements in the past. However, inherent drawbacks, including systemic toxicity and high recurrence rates. Consequently, The nanoparticles, with their targeting and multifunctionality, have captured significant attention. This work synthesizes HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI (HMNPs:Fe3O4@SiO2@mSiO2-C18) nanoparticles, integrating diverse functionalities for enhanced cancer therapy. The inclusion of SiO2 and C60 significantly improves the loading efficiency of gallic acid (GA). The magnetic properties of HMNPs enable efficient targeting to cancer sites under external magnetic fields. The -SS- linkage ensures drug release exclusively in tumor cells over-expressing glutathione (GSH), minimizing adverse effects on normal tissues. Introduction of Polyethyleneimine (PEI) imparts positive charge to facilitate accumulation on negatively charged tumor surfaces and enhances biocompatibility. Additionally, the fluorescence properties of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) facilitate real-time monitoring of material distribution in vivo during mouse and clinical therapy. Furthermore, the nano-magnetic targeting material demonstrates efficient drug loading and precise control over drug release, addressing issues such as the poor biocompatibility and high biotoxicity associated with traditional materials. This study not only achieves effective tumor treatment but also offers a promising direction for developing efficient multifunctional nano-targeting materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Traditional cancer treatments are hindered by high recurrence rates, eradication challenges, and significant side effects, underscoring the critical need for innovative therapeutic strategies [1–6]. Nano-scale drug delivery systems represent a promising avenue in targeted cancer therapy, offering advantages such as stable drug delivery, reduced toxicity, prolonged circulation times, and enhanced water solubility of anti-cancer drugs [7,8]. Stimuli-responsive systems, leveraging internal triggers like temperature, pH, enzyme activity, and glutathione, have emerged as effective tools for precise drug delivery and release [9,10]. These systems exhibit precise drug capture in response to physiological changes in vivo [11], with particle morphology playing a crucial role in cellular uptake and preventing phagocytosis [12].Nanostructures of iron oxides, particularly iron magnetic fluids such as Fe3O4 and Fe2O3, offer promising properties for tumor therapy, including hyperthermia, drug delivery, nanomedicine, magnetic resonance imaging, and magnetic cell separation[13–17].Fabrication techniques enable the synthesis of materials with diverse functionalities. Composite materials with sandwich structures, synthesized through compression molding (CM) or vacuum-assisted material extrusion, exemplify one approach to enhancing overall performance [18–20]. Spherical nanoparticles, particularly influenced by size during drug release, offer numerous advantages in targeted cancer therapy [21–23] by leveraging the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect for selective tumor accumulation, prolonged circulation times, and customizable surface properties for specific targeting [24].

The biocompatible C60 molecule, with dimensions suitable for drug delivery, can be served as the promising carriers for small molecule drugs such as gambogic acid (GA) [25]. C60 and its derivatives, characterized by low biological toxicity and extended persistence post-injection, hold potential for effective drug delivery. Polyethyleneimine (PEI), forming stable complexes with GA through electrostatic adsorption, demonstrates high affinity and phagocytosis rates in cancer cells, leading to significant anti-tumor effects [26]. Furthermore, PEI-based nanomaterials find utility as contrast agents in molecular imaging, facilitating tissue and disease imaging [27]. The incorporation of disulfide bond-coupled small molecule prodrug nanoparticles enhances carrier steric flexibility, particularly in constructing redox-responsive systems. These nanoparticles, stable in systemic circulation, undergo reduction to sulfhydryl groups within tumor cells under high concentrations of glutathione (GSH), thereby improving the hydrophilicity of the drug-loading system [28]. Gambogic acid (GA), known for its diverse bioactivities including anti-tumor effects across various cancer types, remains underexplored in inhibiting SMMC-7721 cell growth, possibly due to its unique mechanism linked to telomerase activity inhibition [29,30].

This study presents the development of spherical multifunctional carriers incorporating HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI. Notably, the synergistic integration of C60 and PEI enhances the drug loading capacity of nanomaterials, while the inclusion of N-GQDs enables real-time fluorescence imaging [31]. These carriers are characterized by their minimal cytotoxicity, exceptional drug loading efficiency, and sustained release profile for gambogic acid (GA). Moreover, this study investigates the efficacy of GA-loaded multifunctional carriers in inhibiting SMMC-7721 cells, both in vitro and in vivo, thus contributing to the advancement of targeted cancer therapy.
Comprehensive Synthesis of HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI Nanomaterials, Drug Release, Cellular and Animal Experiments—Please refer to the Supporting Information for Detailed Procedures.
The cell and mouse experiments study were approved by Shanghai University, Baoshan 200444, China. SMMC-7721 (CL-0216) were kindly provided by Procell Life Science & Technology Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology analysis of composite mesoporous particles
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI nanoparticles designed for targeted cancer therapy, along with the pyrolysis induction process, is delineated in Scheme 1. The SEM for α-Fe2O3, α-Fe2O3@SiO2, HMNPs-C60 and HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI are presented in Fig. S1a-d. The TEM for HMNPs, HMNPs-C60, HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs and HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI are shown in Fig. S1e-h. The core–shell structure of α-Fe2O3@SiO2 is demonstrated in Fig. 1 a, where a core composed of α-Fe2O3 with a diameter of approximately 50–60 nm is uniformly enveloped by a SiO2 shell measuring 70–90 nm in thickness, as indicated in the inset of Fig. 1a. Fe2O3@SiO2@mSiO2-C18 was calcined at 550℃ in a tubular furnace, resulting in the formation of HMNPs with enhanced magnetic characteristics. Fig. 1b illustrates the the spherical HMNPs with an approximate diameter of 160–170 nm. The iron core has a thickness of approximately 55 nm, accompanied by a cavity thickness of approximately 10 nm, while a clearly observed silica shell with a thickness of 40 nm completes the structure. HRSEM images in Fig. 1c-e depict HMNPs, HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs, and HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI, respectively. Additionally, Fig. 1e presents the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping analysis, which uniformly reveals the distribution of S, C, O, Si, Fe, and N in the mesoporous particles, confirming the successful preparation of the HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI carriers.

Fig. 2 a and b, along with Tables S1, demonstrate the characteristics of the spherical HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI. The material exhibits a surface area of 553.25 m2/g and a pore size of 3.79 nm. These results indicate that the spherical material possess a larger surface area, making them highly suitable for efficient drug loading.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zeta potential analysis of composite mesoporous particles
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2c reveals notable distinctions in coercivity and residual magnetization among spherical HMNPs, HMNPs-C60, HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs, HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS and HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI. As the modifiers increase, the saturation magnetization gradually decreases, with HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI exhibiting a saturation magnetization of 3.9 emu/g. These magnetic properties highlight the potential utility of composite mesoporous material particles as contrast agents. Furthermore, spherical materials exhibit remarkable fluorescence and magnetic attributes, as depicted in Fig. 2d and e. The fluorescence intensity of HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI at 3.8 × 106 underscores its enhanced suitability for in vivo fluorescence imaging.

The feasibility of the nano-drug loading system for cellular uptake was evaluated using zeta potential analysis. As depicted in Fig. 2f, significant alterations in the zeta potential of the materials were observed. HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI materials exhibited a positive charge of 25.31 mV. Considering that cell membranes typically possess a negative charge, the positive charge of the composite nanomaterials suggests the potential of HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI as an effective drug carrier.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural analysis of composite mesoporous particles
ce:section.ce:para: The elemental composition of the carrier was analyzed via XPS, and the results are presented in Table S2. Fig. 3 a-e illustrates the total spectrum and the peak spectra of C, O, N, and S for HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS, providing further evidence to support the conclusions drawn from EDS mapping analysis. The structural analysis of the carrier was conducted using XRD, as depicted in Fig. 3f. HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI exhibits an amorphous broad diffraction peak around 2θ = 20°, indicative of the presence of non-crystalline particles. Additionally, distinct characteristic diffraction peaks are observed at 2θ = 30.11°, 35.47°, 43.12°, 53.48°, 57.01°, 62.59°, corresponding to crystal planes (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), (440), respectively, confirming the presence of Fe3O4 in the synthesized product. The intensity of these characteristic diffraction peaks gradually decreases due to surface grafting with C60, N-GQDs, -SS-, and PEI on HMNPs. However, the crystal structure remains unchanged, indicating the stability of the synthesized/modified composite mesoporous microparticles in the crystal phase.

The IR spectrum of the spherical material was examined, and the results are illustrated in Fig. S2a-f. Two bands at 1091 cm−1 and 463 cm−1, corresponding to Si-O-Si vibrations, were observed, indicating the presence of SiO2 shells on the surface of HMNPs. The successful grafting of amino groups (–NH2) into the mesoporous channels of HMNPs is supported by C-H bending and stretching vibrations at around 2920 cm−1. The successful grafting of carboxylated C60 on HMNPs is confirmed by the appearance of two absorption bands at 1709 and 1550 cm−1, denoting the characteristic peaks of amide I and amide II bands. The presence of the characteristic peak at 953 cm−1 further confirms the grafting of N-C60. Additionally, Fig. S2d shows the characteristic peaks of N-H, C = C, and C-H in N-GQDs at 3440, 1455, and 1391 cm−1, respectively, indicating the successful grafting of N-GQDs onto HMNPs-C60. The presence of the characteristic peak at 560 cm−1 in Fig. S2e confirms the grafting of 3,3-dithiodipropionic anhydride (-SS-) onto HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs, resulting in the formation of HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS. Finally, the successful grafting of PEI is evidenced by the appearance of characteristic peaks at 2974 and 1052 cm−1 in Fig. S2f, indicating the presence of tertiary amine in PEI. The collective infrared spectrum analysis results confirm the successful preparation of HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and sustained release properties of composite mesoporous microparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Effects of grafted molecule on GA encapsulation rate and cumulative sustained release rate are illustrated in Fig. S3. Fig. 4 presents the outcomes of a simulated study investigating the cumulative sustained release rate of GA in both normal cells and tumor cells, employing spherical carriers of HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI under the optimal dosage and pH/redox dual response were analyzed. As depicted in Fig. 4a, the initial drugs release rates in the four experiments groups were relatively rapid within the first 10 h. Subsequently, a gradual decrease in drug release rate ensued, transitioning into a slow-release stage over time. This phenomenon arises from the reversible interaction between the GA drug and the carrier, facilitating the sustained and controlled release of encapsulated molecules. [32]. After 140 h, drug release reached a plateau, primarily attributed to diffusion from the cavity and shell mesoporous channels within HMNPs. Two pivotal factors, namely bonding interactions with PEI via the carboxyl group of GA and π-π stacking interaction between GA molecules and C60, modulate the drug carrier interactions, thereby promoting slow drug release [33].

Analysis of Fig. 4b and c reveals that the carriers featuring the -SS- bond display elevated cell mortality. This effect stems from the cleavage of the -SS- bond by over-expressed GSH in cancer cells, leading to augmented drug release. Remarkably, the heightened cell mortality associated with spherical materials is ascribed to the lateral migration of spherical nanoparticles toward blood vessels within the microenvironment, thereby facilitating increased cellular uptake.

The carrier, engineered with C60, N-GQDs, and PEI, exhibits a substantial enhancement in the encapsulation rate and sustained release rate of GA compared to HMNPs alone. As illustrated in Fig. 4d and e, the encapsulation efficiency and cumulative sustained release rate of the spherical carrier increased from 47.44 % and 29.72 % for the initial HMNPs to 80.18 % and 45.20 % for the final HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI, respectively. The combined effect of physical loading of GA by C60 and HMNPs, along with the chemical bonding of GA by N-GQDs and PEI, synergistically enhances the loading capacity of HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI for GA. This dual loading approach significantly augments the carrier's overall drug-loading capabilities. Within tumor cells, various mechanisms including -SS- bond cleavage, electrostatic repulsion between N-GQDs and PEI, and GA interactions collectively promote drug release. This multifaceted strategy enhances the cumulative sustained release rate of the drug, establishing the HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI carrier as a promising platform for controlled drug delivery in cancer treatment.

The effects of varying amounts of GA on the encapsulation and drug loading efficiency of HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs and HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI were investigated to ascertain the optimal GA dosage. In the experiment, 10 mg of HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs were loaded with varying amounts of GA, and the results for encapsulation efficiency and drug loading are presented in Table S3. Similarly, the GA encapsulation efficiency and drug loading efficiency of HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI are shown in Table S4. Due to the abundant amino groups in PEI, it facilitates the interaction with GA via electrostatic interaction, thereby enhancing drug loading. As the amount of GA increased from 1.0 mg to 4.0 mg, drug loading gradually increased, while the encapsulation efficiency showed no significant changes. However, encapsulation efficiency exhibited a notable decline when the GA amount increased to 8.0 mg, attributed to the phenomenon of overloading, where an excessive material leads to a decreased encapsulation efficiency [34]. To achieve maximum utilization of the carrier for drug loading, the optimal GA dosage was determined to be 4.0 mg. At this dosage, the encapsulation efficiency reached 54.15 %, with a corresponding drug loading of 216.59 mg/g. This optimal amount ensures efficient drug loading while maintaining a high encapsulation efficiency.

The influence of varying pH and GSH concentrations on drug release was investigated. As depicted in Table 1 , the cumulative release rate of the spherical system HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI under pH = 5.7 + GSH condition surpasses that of other conditions. This phenomenon can be attributed to the acidic milieu and elevated GSH levels typically observed within tumor cells. The presence of GSH catalyzes the cleavage of disulfide bonds (-SS-) within the carrier structure, thereby exposing more pores and facilitating the release of encapsulated drugs. Furthermore, under acidic conditions, GA undergoes protonation, leading to electrostatic repulsion between GA and the positively charged HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI carrier. This repulsion promotes the detachment of GA molecules from the carrier, thereby enhancing drug release. Consequently, this system enables dual-controlled drug release based on the pH and redox conditions characteristic of tumor cells, while sparing normal cells. The spherical material exhibits a heightened sustained release rate of 45.17 %, prolonging drug release and reducing the frequency of administration. This characteristic holds promise for minimizing potential side effects in patients, rendering it a valuable asset for practical tumor-targeted therapies.

The cytotoxicity of the carrier was assessed using the MTT method, and the results depicted in Fig. S4 demonstrate the reduced cytotoxicity of the spherical carrier. To further validate the oxidative controlled release performance of the -SS- bond, a cytotoxicity study was performed on HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs (GA) and HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI (GA).

Additionally, the release behavior of the drug from the carrier was investigated using the Higuchi, first-order kinetic, and Weibull models, and the results are presented in Fig. S5. Among these models, the Weibull model was found to better capture the release behavior of GA over time.

To investigate the cellular uptake of composite nanoparticles, the confocal laser method was employed. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, the red fluorescence corresponds to the staining of the cell membrane with DiD dye at an excitation wavelength of 633 nm, while the blue fluorescence represents the N-GQDs within the material at a wavelength of 440 nm. The merged image reveals the envelopment of the blue fluorescence by the red, indicative of the strong targeting capability of the spherical materials, as evidenced by the heightened fluorescence intensity. This observation underscores the efficient cellular internalization of the composite nanoparticles, thereby emphasizing their potential in targeted drug delivery.

In vivo experiments were conducted on tumor-bearing mice, with SMMC-7721 cells transplanted, to assess the inhibitory effect of the magnetic nano-drug delivery system on tumors growth. As depicted in Fig. 5d, tumors in the control group treated with PBS exhibited typical growth, while the administration group demonstrated a reduction in relative tumor volume as the treatment progressed. This decrease in tumor volume can be attributed to the magnetic field, which facilitates the accumulation of the magnetic nano-drug loading system within the tumor site and subsequent release of the drug within the tumor microenvironment. Furthermore, Fig. 5b provides a visual representation of tumor extraction from mice at four-day intervals, highlighting the gradual reduction in tumor volume achieved through magnetic targeting and the dual-response systems. By the 13th day, the relative volume of tumors in the spherical drug-loading system group was measured at 0.023. This observation indicates that the spherical carrier materials are effectively internalized by tumor cells and display desirable pharmacokinetic properties, ultimately leading to an efficient inhibition of tumor growth. Collectively, these findings support the potential of the magnetic nano-drug delivery system, particularly in combination with spherical carrier materials, as a promising approach for targeted drug delivery and controlled release, thus offering effective treatment strategies for tumors.

Fig. 5c provides a comparative analysis of the heart, spleen, and lung tissues between the control group administered with PBS and the experimental group that underwent magnetic targeting. Notably, no abnormal lesions were observed in either groups. Examination of the tissue sections revealed that the cardiac tissue exhibited a smooth and well-organized heart muscle, with centrally located nuclei within the cells. In the spleen tissue sections, the purple-red area represented the red pulp, while blue dots indicated the distribution of the white pulp. The loose lymphoid tissue was observed between the red and white pulp regions. Similarly, the lung tissue sections displayed circularly arranged muscle fibers surrounding the alveolar openings, indicative of normal lung tissue morphology. These findings suggest that the synthesized spherical nano-drug delivery systems effectively inhibited liver cancer cells without causing damage to surrounding tissues and organs. Furthermore, the slender morphological features of the spherical drug-loading system played a significant role in reducing nonspecific uptake by cell. Overall, this highlights the potential of the developed nano-drug delivery system for targeted cancer therapy, with minimal off-target effects on healthy tissues and organs.

Using the medical pulsed magnetic field generator YMC-11, magnetic targeting intervention experiments were conducted on mice injected with HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI (GA)-99Tcm via the posterior auricular vein. By adjusting the voltage and frequency of the medical pulsed magnetic field generator, the intensity and gradient of the magnetic field could be modulated.

In the SMMC-7721 tumor magnetic targeting intervention experiment, approximately 37 MBq (approximately 1 mCi) of the labeled compound (HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI (GA)-99Tcm) was directly injected into the SMMC-7721 tumor in the leg. SPECT imaging analysis was performed 30 min after magnetic targeting intervention at the site of the SMMC-7721 tumor-bearing mouse's left hind limb (thigh tumor).

Based on the ROI calculations in Fig. 6 , the radioactive counts of the tumor site on the left side were measured as 112223, while those on the right side were 42861. The ratio of radioactive activity between the left and right sides was calculated to be 2.61, indicating an increase in radioactive activity at the site of magnetic targeting intervention compared to the contralateral side.

Therefore, it is concluded that under magnetic targeting intervention, the soft tissue uptake ability of HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI (GA) is approximately 2.6 times higher compared to that without targeted intervention. This strongly supports the targeted effect of magnetic targeting intervention on tissue at the SMMC-7721 tumor site. The comprehensive performance of the HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI nano-material is depicted in Table S5. Future efforts will focus on optimizing its structural properties to enhance saturation magnetization, thereby enabling effective synergistic magnetic targeting for both passive and active therapies.

The study aims to enhance cellular uptake, mitigate biotoxicity, increase drug loading capacity, prolong sustained release time, and enable responsiveness to endogenous stimuli within the tumor microenvironment. To achieve these objectives, magnetic mesoporous silica served as the foundational matrix, onto which sequential modifications of C60, N-GQDs, and PEI were applied to enhance drug loading capacity. The incorporation of -SS- functionality endowed the carrier with specific responsiveness to GSH present in tumor cells, culminating in the development of a magnetic fluorescent nano-drug delivery system capable of integrated diagnosis and therapy, termed spherical HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI. These drug-loaded systems exhibited favorable fluorescence and magnetic properties. Utilizing GA as the model drug, cumulative sustained release rates of 45.17 % were observed for spherical HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI under pH = 5.7 + GSH conditions. Cytotoxicity assays and confocal microscopy images demonstrated low cytotoxicity of the drug-loading systems and their ability to penetrate the cytoplasm and nucleus of SMMC-7721 cells via endocytosis. Furthermore, in vitro anti-tumor experiments revealed promising inhibitory effects of spherical HMNPs-C60-N-GQDs-SS-PEI on tumor cells, indicating its potential for targeted therapeutic applications in cancer treatment. However, further investigation is needed to enhance the overall morphology and saturation magnetization of the materials, which can be achieved through adjusting reaction conditions optimizing material structure and synthesizing multifunctional nano-materials.

Wei-wei Huan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Mengyang Dong: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Ziling Chang: Investigation. Huafei Li: Validation. Wei Liu: Validation. Yuxiang Yang: Software, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Hongmin Yuan: Project administration, Software. Yan Huang: Methodology. Wenwen Liu: Methodology. Riccardo Carlini: Writing – review & editing. Mo Chen: Writing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (20577010, 20971043), the Open Project Program of the State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, Jilin University (2023-8), the Zhejiang Public Welfare Public Research Program (LGC22B010001), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32201238, 32371809) and the Three agricultures and nine agricultural related institutions” Agricultural Science and Technology Cooperation Plan Project of Zhejiang Province (2022SNJF059).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2024.112911.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
